reasoning: After assigning more constrained words, 'apple' was one of the few nouns left. Group (L) [23, 19, 24] shows signs for the Cistercian habit ('cowl', 'scapular') followed by a sign for 'eat'. This compound 'Cistercian-eat' was matched to 'apple'.

Groups (A) [13, 30, 4] and (D) [21, 30, 4] represent places, likely 'barn' and 'bathroom'. (D) uses sign 21 ('monk'), fitting for a monastery bathroom. (A) uses sign 13 ('cowl-person', associated with Benedictines), which is plausibly matched with 'barn', a place for agricultural work associated with that order.

Groups (A) and (D) are parallel structures for places. (D) [21, 30, 4] combines the sign for 'monk' (21, from 'a Cistercian monk') with a sign for a place [30, 4]. 'Bathroom' is a logical place specific to monks in a monastery.

Group (N) [13, 14] was identified as 'Benedictines'. Sign 13 shows a hand by the ear, representing a cowl, and sign 14 points to a black sleeve. Benedictines are known as the 'black monks', so 'cowl' + 'black' is a direct description.
Group (T) [8, 9] is a component of group (O) 'Christmas'. Sign 8 (hand up) means 'blessed' and sign 9 (to mouth) means 'bread'. 'Blessed bread' is the definition of 'the Blessed Sacrament'.
Group (M) [26, 12] shares sign 12 ('sweet') with 'queen bee' and 'chocolate milk'. Sign 26 shows a hand at the mouth, meaning 'eat' or 'food'. 'Sweet food' is a perfect description of 'cake'.
Group (Q) [28, 12] shares sign 12 ('sweet'). By elimination of other 'sweet' words, this must be 'chocolate milk'. Therefore, sign 28 represents 'chocolate'.

Group (O) [18, 8, 9] contains the signs for 'the Blessed Sacrament' [8, 9]. The additional sign, 18, depicts 'birth'. Since Christmas is the celebration of the birth of Jesus (represented by the Blessed Sacrament), 'birth' + 'Blessed Sacrament' translates to 'Christmas'.

Group (H) [21, 22, 20, 29] is a long compound sign. Sign 21 means 'monk' and sign 22 points to a white sleeve. Cistercians are known as the 'white monks', making this a clear sign for 'a Cistercian monk'.
Group (J) consists of a single sign, 17. The gesture is a hand on the top of the head, indicating the tonsure, a shaved head style specific to monks. This is a very direct sign for 'Cistercians'.
Group (B) [5, 17] combines sign 5 ('sleep', hand at head) with sign 17 ('Cistercians'). The 'Cistercians' sleeping place' is the 'dormitory'.
Group (F) [1, 6] contains group (G) [6], which was determined to be 'milk'. Sign 1 is a clear 'drinking' motion. Therefore, 'drink-milk' is the sign for the general verb '(to) drink'.
Group (P) [19, 10] ends with sign 10, hypothesized to mean 'land'. Sign 19 shows holding lapels, a stereotypical gesture for an 'English gentleman'. Thus, it represents 'England'.

Group (I) [15, 7, 3] contains group (U) [15, 7], which was identified as 'snow'. 'Ice' can be described as 'hard snow' or 'snow on the ground'. Sign 3 means 'ground/hard'. Therefore, 'snow' + 'ground/hard' signifies 'ice'.
Group (E) [16, 8, 16, 3] combines signs for 'cold/white' (16), 'blessed' (8), and 'ground/land' (3). The combination 'blessed land of white/cold' is a fitting poetic description for 'Iceland'.
Group (V) [20, 10] ends with sign 10, hypothesized to mean 'land'. Sign 20 is making a cross on the chest, a clear reference to Catholicism and the Vatican, thus representing 'Italy'.
Group (G) is a single sign, 6. It is the base for group (F) '(to) drink'. Given the pair, 'milk' is the most logical base noun. The sign is at the jowl, which could be related to a cow or goat.

Group (W) [13, 30] was matched to 'a nun' by elimination. It contains sign 13 ('cowl-person'), which is clearly related to a religious figure. After assigning the other words in its group [(A),(D),(K)], 'a nun' was the only remaining personal noun.
Group (C) [9, 31] combines sign 9 ('bread') with sign 31 (two fingers on lips, 'word/speech'). 'Bread-word' is a good metaphor for 'poetry' as nourishment for the soul.

Group (S) [27, 12] is part of the 'sweet' group (sharing sign 12). Sign 27 is a hand on the forehead, indicating a 'crown' or 'queen'. 'Queen' + 'sweet' (from honey) logically translates to 'queen bee'.
Group (U) [15, 7] is the base form of group (I) 'ice'. Therefore, it represents 'snow'.
Group (R) [1, 2, 3] combines the signs for 'drink/water' (1), 'pour' (2), and 'ground' (3). This sequence describes how a tree gets its nourishment, making it a logical sign for 'tree'.

Group (K) [5, 30] was the last of its structural set to be assigned. By elimination, it must correspond to 'wooden table'. The logic is that sign 5 ('sleep') and sign 30 ('table') combine to mean 'table for resting things', i.e., a wooden table. This is a weak link, but forced by all other, stronger matches.
The sign for 'Christmas' (O) is [18, 8, 9]. Since [8, 9] means 'the Blessed Sacrament' (representing Jesus), sign 18 must mean 'birth'. 'Birth' is the core concept of 'baby'.
The sign for 'tree' (R) is [1, 2, 3], which was decoded as 'water-pour-ground'. The sign 2 is a distinct pouring motion, thus it means '(to) pour'.

The sign for 'Iceland' (E) is [16, 8, 16, 3]. Sign 16 is a downward fluttering motion with the fingers, an iconic sign for precipitation. Since 'snow' is already assigned to another sign group (U), 16 represents 'rain'.

The sign for 'a Cistercian monk' (H) is [21, 22, 20, 29]. Sign 29 shows fingers moving away from the mouth, which can represent breath, spirit, or steam. Tea is a hot, steaming beverage, so 29 is the most plausible sign for 'tea'.
